source: courses/Biology/Immunology/Lectures/H. Organization and expression of antibody genes.pptx
subject: Biology

segments.text:
Important Points
Functional antibody genes do not exist as such in embryonic DNA, but are created during development
During development, cells of the immune system rearrange genes that they need to function.
Rearranged genes can be joined in a sloppy fashion, further increasing diversity
Somatic Mutation of immunoglobulin genes even further enhances diversity

Human Immunodiversity
Total number of
different genes in human       ~21,000
Number of
different antibody Molecules
~1,000,000,000,000
(2.14 vs 1012)

Antibody diversity is generated in part by combinations of different heavy and light chains.
Pretend
1000 different heavy chain genes
1000 different light chain genes
Would yield 106 different antibodies
-nowhere near enough
Any heavy chain can bind to any light chain

Myeloma cell
Cancerous plasma cell that still makes antibody.  Because the cell constantly divides, but still only produces one type of antibody, they are extremely useful for obtaining large amounts of a specific antibody and for studying individual antibody gene arrangement.

Radioactive Probes:
Variable region            Constant Region
Tonegawa used mouse k light chain

Size fractionate molecules by gel electrophoresis
Transfer or “blot” molecules to a solid support (nitrocellulose or nylon membrane)
Use “probes” that stick specifically to molecules of interest to “light them up”
Need to know:
Southern blot-DNA
Northern blot-RNA
Western blot-Protein
Important Technology
Embryonic Immunoglobulin DNA is different than differentiated cell DNA

In contrast to most somatic cells, cells of the immune system re-arrange their DNA.
Not all DNA is re-arranged, just DNA that is used to make immune system specific proteins
The re-arrangement occurs during B-cell development.

Human k coding sequences on chromosome 2p11
Diversity resulting from DNA recombination:
40V units x 5 J regions =200 different antibody proteins

Example of k light chain locus after DNA recombination
Variable       Constant

22
k and l light chain genomic segments differ with respect to JC arrangement
k: 40V 5J 1C
l: 30V 4J 4C

Heavy chain synthesis requires two recombination events
Regulated accessibility also likely contributes to the ordered rearrangement of IgH and TCRb genes, wherein D-to-J rearrangements proceed to completion before the onset of V-to-DJ rearrangements (Alt et al., 1984; Born et al., 1985; Sleckman et al., 2000).
Generation of functional Heavy Chain gene

GERMLINE ORGANIZATION OF HUMAN IG HEAVY CHAIN AND LIGHT CHAIN LOCI
Lambda light chain locus
Gene segments
30 (V), 4 (J) and 4 (C)
Kappa light chain locus
Gene segments
40 (V), 5 (J) and 1 (C)
Heavy chain locus
Gene segments
65 (V), 27 (D), 6 (J) and 9 (C)
Antibody gene arrangement in the mouse

RSS- Recombination signal sequences
Recombination has to occur between different elements :so called 12/23 or one-turn/ two-turn rule

Recombination is catalyzed by developing lymphocyte specific protein
Recombination Activating Genes
Rag1 and Rag2
Are expressed during B
cell development
Both are necessary
Induce double strand breaks
During recombination nucleotide changes are also introduced
“P” comes from “palindromic”
During recombination nucleotide changes are also introduced

Terminal deoxynucleotidyl Transferase (Tdt) adds up to 15 nucleotides that are non template, or N-nucleotides
P
P
N

(Mature, but still naïve)
B-Cells exit to periphery with re-arranged receptors and are “naïve”
Allelic exclusion

Allelic Exclusion
A process that permits expression of only one of the allelic forms of a gene.  For example, a B-cell expresses only one allele for an antibody heavy chain and one allele for a light chain.

3-D view of Light chain
Flattened view of Light chain
Sequence view of Highly variable part of Light chain
CDR- complementarity determining regions
(hypervarible)
Somatic hypermutation occurs in the periphery

Somatic Hypermutation
The induced increase of mutation, 103 to 106 fold over the background rate, in the regions in and around rearranged immunoglobulin genes.  In animals such as humans and mice, somatic hypermutation occurs in germinal centers.

Novel Mechanisms for Generating
Immune System Diversity
Site-specific Recombination
Imprecise Joining
Somatic Hypermutation (affinity maturation)

Transcriptional Regulation of Immunoglobulin Genes
Note: rearrangement brings promoter within range of enhancer
Enhancer: cis-acting elements that can regulate transcription from nearby genes.  Can be situated before the gene, after it, in the middle or on either DNA strand
Class switching
Heavy chain class switching occurs in the periphery
myc is a Transcription factor, first found as a Tumor virus Oncogene

Oops.
Improper recombination can result in activation of proto-oncogene

Burkitts Lymphoma
B-cell lymphoma
Usually treated with chemotherapy

Important Terms/Concepts
k, l, and Heavy chains
Variable, Joining, Diversity and Constant Regions
Light chains: VJC
k V40 J5  C1
Heavy Chains VDJC
RSS
Site-specific Recombination
RAGs, 12/23 or 1-turn/two-turn Rule
Imprecise Joining
Somatic mutation (affinity maturation)
Allelic Exclusion
Class switching
Burkitt’s Lymphoma

Activation Induced cytidine Deaminase (AID)
Note: involved in Class Switching Recombination (CSR) and Somatic Hyper Mutation (SHM)
Numerous cytokines generate signals for proliferation and class switching
Activation Induced cytidine Deaminase (AID)
